text.title: I Travelled To Afghanistan... And Left Within 3 hours
text.u: Okay, here goes nothing. This trip was
stepping out of my comfort zone. I was
on my way to Afghanistan, a country now
ruled and run by the Taliban,
statistically the most dangerous country
in the world and a place which is just
advised not to go. [music] But I wanted
to see the real side of Afghanistan, not
just what you hear online. The idea was
to spend 5 days there. No bad agenda
whatsoever. just me traveling around,
meeting locals, trying the [music] food,
but it didn't go to plan. I left the
country within 24 hours of being there.
Let me let me explain why. But just
before I want to clarify, this is not
like a hate video. No one is to blame
but myself for this. You know, I made
the decision to enter someone else's
country where they have their own rules
and regulations and everything that led
up to it was ultimately my decision. I'm
my own person and I I take
responsibility for it. Anyway, with all
that out of the way, let me take you to
the very start of the trip where
everything started to unfold
in Istanbul, which is our connection to
Kabul, the capital of Afghanistan.
That's where we're going. After a few
hours of waiting, we boarded the final
plane, a 4-hour flight from Istanbul to
Kabul.
As the flight got near, I remember this
being the most nervous I'd been. Are you
getting nervous?
Um, yeah, a little bit. I'm going to be
honest. Yeah. Yeah. Same. I just don't
know like what to expect when I get to
the airport. You know what I mean?
[music] Eventually, the flight pulled
in. We've made it.
I walked through the airport into
airport security. And at this point, I'm
now officially on their land. So, nerves
were even higher. I started to overthink
everything. And as I walked up to the
airport border control,
>> no, just see see the place. Look around.
>> They were completely fine. There was no
issues whatsoever.
>> Thank you, bro.
>> As we were getting to the final security
checkpoint, a member of Taliban came
over to us.
>> Excuse me. Hello. How are you?
>> How you doing? Good.
>> Nice to meet you. I'm Joe.
>> Nice to meet you. Where do we go first?
>> We go we stay in Kabul for a bit.
>> Okay.
>> And then we go see those lakes.
>> Kabul too.
>> Are we leaving? Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.
>> Good to meet you guys.
>> Have a nice trip.
>> He was super friendly towards us and
[music] that continued like leaving the
airport. All the locals were starting
conversations with us. They were asking
if we needed help, offering us to use
their phones. Like the hospitality was
amazing. We've made it. Welcome to
Afghanistan.
How you doing?
>> You good, Joe? Nice to meet you. We met
up with our tour guide, Manm [music]
Moon, and he was honestly such a sound,
kind, genuine guy. I'm a travel guide in
Afghanistan. And now I'm busy with the
nice guys. [music]
[music]
>> We first got checked into the hotel.
>> Very nice.
>> Really? Nice to meet you. How you doing?
Nice to meet you. Look at this. This is
beautiful. How you doing?
>> You good? Nice to meet you, man. Nice to
meet you, man.
>> Thank you very much. Thank you.
>> Amazing, man. Thank you very much. Nice
to meet you.
>> Here we are. As the flight was pulling
in, I was pretty [music] nervous, I'll
be honest. And as soon as like I spoke
to the first person that worked at the
airport, my nerves went like so far.
Everyone has been so nice. My moon told
us to change into the [music] local
clothing. This one is
This is what we've been told to wear.
And Jack is I rate it.
>> Jack is a fan. You
>> like it?
>> Let's head out into the city of Kabul.
>> The first location we came to [music]
was this giant Taliban flag. And as I'm
sure you can imagine, there were tons of
Taliban around it.
>> The camera there.
>> Again, they were all incredibly friendly
towards us.
>> How are you?
>> We're good, man. Very good.
>> Thank you. Alice, that is crazy.
Impressive.
>> We were making good conversation. In
fact, one of them asked for my number
and he sent me like loads of Afghanistan
memes.
You just wouldn't expect that.
But yeah, things were positive at this
point. We were in a we're in a good good
mood.
But this is where things took a slight
Uturn.
As we left that area, we headed to the
streets [music] of Kabal.
>> Where are we now?
>> We are going to go inside of the crime.
>> Okay.
>> We came to this beautiful mosque which
our tour [music] guide recommended and
we wanted to check out. Guarding it,
however, were some officials. Officials
are just like Taliban members, security.
They spotted [music] us instantly and
one of them started searching me.
What is this? It's a microphone.
You see, I was wearing a microphone like
I do whenever [music] I film, and he
didn't like that.
>> Do you have journalist visa?
>> No, I didn't. I only had a tourist visa.
You see, this is a bit of a gray area
because filming a YouTube video on one
hand can be seen as a journalist video,
but on the other hand, it can just be
seen as, you know, making a innocent
tourist YouTube video. Before [music]
the trip, I did a ton of due diligence
on this. Um, I spoke to companies who
have organized trips here before, and
they said, "Look, a tourist visa is
fine. You don't need a journalist visa."
But now I was actually in the country.
That didn't seem to be the case. It it
seemed to be a problem. When the
microphone had been spotted, I could
sense tensions [music]
kind of rose a little bit. The official
was speaking back and forth with my tour
guide. They were making sure that the
[music] camera lenses were fully
blocked. They took our passports and
took photos of them and they basically
just said, "Look, you can't film."
Eventually, my tour guide managed to
deescalate the situation and I just
thought it was best that we got out of
that area. What What happened to those
two YouTubers? Um, so now I think they
are in uh in jail something. Why?
>> Because it's against of the rule of
these guys. I'm talking about these
guys. You mean the officials?
>> So the officials spotted the YouTubers
and then they sent them to jail.
>> Well, that's quite scary.
>> Yeah, of course.
>> How long How long were they put in jail
for?
>> Maybe for 3 months something. 3 months.
>> Yeah. This was not what I wanted to hear
after what had just happened.
Apparently, like very recently, as in
within the last couple days, there were
new rules put in by the Taliban in
regards to filming.
>> And you heard about the YouTubers who
got put in jail.
>> Yeah. Yeah.
>> Why did they get put in jail?
>> They didn't have the permits.
>> They didn't have the right permits.
>> The government, they think between the
tourist people, there are some spies.
>> Spies?
>> Yeah. I was panicking a little bit at
this point because, you know, my
passport's just been scanned and I
technically don't have the right permit.
So, I said to the tour guide, "Look, can
we just go somewhere a bit quieter, a
bit more out the way?"
We are heading now to an amusement park
here in Afghanistan.
As we walked in, it was like a ghost
town, but there was one other person,
another official.
>> Wow, look at this. Hello.
>> Hello. How you doing?
At this point, I didn't know what was
going on. Um the official was speaking
back and forth with my tour guide in the
local language. He was also going on
call and speaking to other people.
>> And I remember just saying to my tour
guide like what what's going on? And his
[snorts] reply is what just scared me
and and it was the main reasons why we
got out of there. I asked what's going
on and he replied they're starting an
investigation on you. When we heard that
like me and my cameraman Jack just
looked at each other obviously thinking
the worst. You said that they they want
to investigate us.
>> Yeah.
>> Okay. That's not good. All right. I
think um I think me and Jack we might we
might get a flight home just because if
we're not allowed to video like
there's probably not much point and we
don't want to risk going to jail and all
of that. You know what I mean? So we
might have to buy a flight today, get
and get out of here.
Just I just don't want to take the risk.
You know, you telling me about the
YouTubers who got put in jail. Like we
don't want to we don't want to risk that
>> as a normal tourist. You can film your
>> Yeah. But we just don't want to risk it.
>> Yeah.
>> Anyway, we got back to the hotel and we
spoke to our travel guide on his opinion
on what we should do.
>> Just just go just let's get out of
there.
>> Yeah.
>> You think?
>> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. 100% 100%.
>> I want to say look, there's a very high
likelihood we would have been completely
fine. I just didn't want to take the
risk.
>> I don't think we want to push it push it
to the limit where it ends up coming
back and biting us.
>> So, we booked the next available flight
which was early that next morning.
You nervous
>> a little bit. I I'm just like they're
going to wonder why are we leaving so
soon?
>> That's Yeah.
>> And then if we are flagged up plus
leaving so soon like that's not a good
look.
>> No, exactly. That's what I'm thinking.
>> Okay, off we go. On the way to the
airport, I was worrying a little bit
because in my head [music] I was
thinking, one, are they not going to be
suspicious that we're leaving within 24
hours of getting there? And two, if our
passports get scanned, are we going to
flag up on the system [music] because of
what happened the day before? As we made
our way through the airport, the nerves
were very high, but just wanted to act
as [music] calm and composed as
possible. And then I made it to border
control. This was the moment of truth. I
handed him my passport, and as [music]
he scanned it,
we were completely fine. He ushered me
on my way and uh got the flight back.
We're leaved. Now, to end this video, I
want to say, look, there's a very high
likelihood we would have been completely
fine. The people of Afghanistan were
lovely. I genuinely believe if you're
just going there to be a tourist, you'll
be completely fine. There'll be no
issues, but ultimately, we'd gone into
that country. We didn't know if we were
breaking the rules or not, and we just
didn't want to take the risk. It's as
simple as that. Yeah, I'm definitely
going to travel to Afghanistan again at
some point in the future, like
non-filming because I really want to
explore the country. But um that is why
we left Afghanistan so soon after
getting there. Thank you guys for
watching.
